1:
The chapter opens with a date, and the witnesses spend their first labour on it. Keil and Delitzsch insist that "the third year" be reckoned not from the beginning of the drought, as the Rabbins and others assumed, but from the last event named — Elijah's sojourn at Zarephath; and they anchor the whole calculation in the accurate tradition that our Lord and the apostle preserve, that in Ahab's day it did not rain for three years and six months (Luke 4:25; James 5:17). Only by allowing more than two years at Zarephath, they argue, can that span be filled. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott agree on the arithmetic — perhaps six months at the brook Cherith (1 Kings 17) and three years with the widow — and Ellicott adds that the phrase, taken literally, must be counted from the visit to Zarephath.

The grammatical-historical voices also weigh the promise itself. Clarke presses the word haadamah: "I will send rain upon the earth" should read the land or the ground, since the drought probably did not reach beyond the borders of Israel. This is no small point, for it fixes the judgment as a controversy between the Lord and one apostate people, not a natural calamity.

The reformed Gill and the puritan Matthew Henry turn from the clock to the command. God bids Elijah show himself to Ahab only when the hour to favour Israel has come; above two years the prophet had lain hid, and, Henry supposes, his zeal against Baal and his compassion for the people made the confinement long, yet he stirred not till God bade him. Henry draws the pastoral lesson that gives the verse its warmth: "It bodes well to any people when God calls his ministers out of their corners, and bids them show themselves" — itself a sign that he will give rain on the earth, and that we may the better bear the bread of affliction while our eyes see our teachers (Isaiah 30:20-21). What the drought had shut, mercy now prepares to open, and the opening begins with a word to a hidden man.
2:
The single sentence — Elijah went to show himself unto Ahab — is read by the witnesses as an act of pure obedience under mortal risk, and around it they set the famine that makes the risk plain. John Gill fixes the note of courage: the going "showed his cheerful and ready obedience to the will of God, and his great courage and magnanimity, to face a king enraged against him, and that sought his life." The prophet does not calculate; the word came, and he went.

The grammatical-historical reading supplies the scale of the crisis. Keil and Delitzsch mark the clause the narrator drops in almost by the way — and there was a sore famine in Samaria — and let it govern all that follows: the capital itself, the seat of the throne, is gripped, so that the king must turn from affairs of state to the mere keeping alive of his beasts. The famine is not a rumour from the countryside but a hunger at the palace gate.

Behind the bare obedience the fuller narrative gathers, and Matthew Henry's comment on the surrounding verses feeds it. Elijah, so long shut up first by the brook Cherith and then at Zarephath, thought it long to be confined to a corner while Baal reigned and Israel starved; yet he waited on the command. Now, at the word, he walks straight toward the man who has hunted him through every kingdom. The obedience is the more striking for its object: he goes not to a friend but to an enemy, not to safety but into the teeth of a search that has ranged the whole earth for his life. The witnesses do not divide here; they agree that the verse is a quiet portrait of faith that fears God more than kings, and that the sore famine in Samaria is the dark background against which that faith moves. The prophet's whole errand — to break the drought by breaking the idolatry — is compressed into this obedient step toward Ahab, and the coming meeting with Obadiah in v.7 is its first fruit.
3:
The narrative pauses to introduce a remarkable man, and the witnesses gather about his name and his position. John Gill notes the office plainly — Obadiah "was the governor of his house," perhaps his steward — and records the Jewish tradition that would identify him with Obadiah the prophet who wrote the small prophecy. But the deeper interest is his character. Adam Clarke reads the verse as its own testimony: "Obadiah feared the Lord greatly — He was a sincere and zealous worshipper of the true God, and his conduct towards the persecuted prophets was the full proof both of his piety and humanity."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the name into the service of the whole scene. Obadiah signifies servant of Jehovah, and "here corresponds to the character of the man." More than that, his very existence exposes the divided house of Ahab: "It is curiously significant of the hesitating and temporising attitude of Ahab, that, while Jezebel is suffered to persecute, a high officer in the court is able to profess openly the service of Jehovah, and secretly to thwart the cruelty of the queen." Ellicott's Ahab is a man whose heart never quite lets go of the true God, yet who is "overborne by the commanding and ruthless nature of Jezebel" — the same halting between two opinions that v.21 will lay before all Israel.

The puritan Matthew Henry dwells longest on the wonder of grace in a hostile place. Here is "one very good man, who was a great man at court," who "feared the Lord greatly" and had feared him "from his youth" (v.12) — early piety ripening into eminent piety. Henry finds it strange on every side: strange that so wicked a king would prefer and keep him, which argues Obadiah's celebrated honesty and fidelity, as Joseph and Daniel were preferred for their fitness; strange that so good a man would accept office in an idolatrous court, which he could do only because he was never required to bow the knee to Baal; and strange that neither reformed the other. The lesson Henry draws is the one that binds all the voices together: "God has his remnant among all sorts, high and low; there were saints in Nero's household, and in Ahab's." The man who is true to God will be faithful to his prince, and need not go out of the world, bad as it is.
4:
This parenthesis tells what Obadiah's fear of God cost and dared, and the witnesses read it as the proof of his piety and as a dark window on Jezebel's reign. John Gill identifies the victims: the prophets Jezebel "cut off" — or "slew," as the Targum has it — were men "brought up in the schools of the prophets, trained up in religious exercises," teachers of the people though not workers of miracles. Against her sword Obadiah "hid a hundred men of the Lord's prophets by fifty in a cave, and fed them with bread and water." Adam Clarke glosses the provision tenderly: "bread and water" here signify "the necessaries of life, of whatsoever kind" — all that a hunted remnant could be kept alive on in a famine.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the persecution in its larger significance. This is the moment "in which for the first time the royal power was placed in distinct antagonism to the prophetic order," and it is "only known by this allusion" — no other record of it survives. He conjectures that it followed hard upon Elijah's denunciation of judgment, and that the prophet's own retirement to Cherith and Zarephath "may have been a means of escape from it." Ellicott then raises the tension that will haunt the chapter: if Elijah's repeated cry, I, even I, alone remain, is to be taken literally, then "Obadiah's merciful interposition must have availed only for a time, or have simply given opportunity of escape." The hundred hidden prophets and the one prophet who thinks himself alone stand in an unresolved counterpoint.

The puritan Matthew Henry makes the verse a study in active goodness. Obadiah "did not think it enough to fear God himself, but, having wealth and power wherewith to do it, he thought himself obliged to assist and countenance others that feared God." Nor did his charity to the prophets excuse him from personal holiness: "he both feared God greatly himself and patronised those that feared him likewise." Henry marvels at the providence: "See how wonderfully God raises up friends for his ministers and people, for their shelter in difficult times, even where one would least expect them." Bread and water were now scarce commodities, "yet Obadiah will find a competence of both for God's prophets, to keep them alive for service hereafter, though now they were laid aside." The verse is thus at once an indictment of the throne and a monument to the courage that hides the church in a cave and feeds it.
5:
The verse turns from the hidden prophets to the desperate errand of the king, and the witnesses read it for both its topography and its terrible irony. John Gill and Adam Clarke handle the ground: Ahab sends Obadiah "unto all fountains of water, and unto all brooks," which Clarke defines as "all marshy or well-watered districts, where grass was most likely to be preserved." The aim is stated with painful plainness — to find grass enough "to save the horses and mules alive, that we lose not all the beasts." The drought has burned the land so bare that the king of Israel must personally quarter it in search of a blade of green.

The puritan Matthew Henry seizes the moral that the scene almost shouts. Providence had ordered it so "that Ahab might, with his own eyes, see how bad the consequences of this judgment were, that so he might be the better inclined to hearken to Elijah." Yet the king learns nothing from what he sees. Henry's indictment is memorable: "Ahab's care was not to lose all the beasts, many being already lost; but he took no care about his soul, not to lose that; he took a deal of pains to seek grass, but none to seek the favour of God, fencing against the effect, but not enquiring how to remove the cause." The whole tragedy of a half-hearted man is in that sentence — energetic about the symptom, idle about the sin.

Henry sharpens the point by a comparison the grammatical-historical reading of the drought supports. The land of Judah lay close by, yet no such complaint of drought is heard there, "for Judah yet ruled with God, and was faithful with the saints and prophets" (Hosea 11:12), so that the Lord "caused it to rain upon one city, and not upon another" (Amos 4:7-8). By that very distinction, Henry says, Israel might plainly have seen the ground of God's controversy; but "they blinded their eyes, and hardened their hearts, and would not see." The witnesses do not debate; together they make Ahab's grass-hunt a parable of the sinner who labours against the judgment while refusing to touch its cause, and who cannot read the plain sermon written across his own scorched fields.
6:
A single half-verse — they divided the land between them, to pass through it, Ahab one way and Obadiah another — is passed over quickly by the witnesses, yet what little they say opens onto the whole crisis. John Gill gives only the plain sense: "one took one part, and the other the other part." But the very fact that the king of Israel and his chief steward must personally split the country between them and tramp it on foot in search of pasture is, for the grammatical-historical reading, the measure of the famine's severity. Keil and Delitzsch preserve the picture the narrator draws: "Ahab went one way by himself, and Obadiah went another way by himself" — no retinue of servants sent in their place, but the two greatest men in the realm reduced to scouring the fountains and brooks alone.

The puritan Matthew Henry, whose long comment gathers up these opening verses, draws the point that this dividing of the land is the hinge on which the meeting turns. It is by this errand — and by this route agreed between them — that Obadiah is brought into the very path where Elijah waits. The prophet, Henry says, "knew, by the Spirit, where to meet him"; the king's own desperate search for grass becomes, unknown to him, the providential arrangement by which he is brought within reach of the man from whose word, at God's mouth, rain must come. The same drought that scatters the two men to opposite quarters of the land steers one of them straight to the prophet.

So the verse, thin as it is, is not idle. It shows a kingdom on its knees before the judgment it will not confess, a king who spends his strength "fencing against the effect, but not enquiring how to remove the cause," and a providence quietly at work in the mere logistics of a search-party. What Ahab thinks a hunt for fodder, God has appointed as the road to Carmel. The next step of that road is the meeting itself, when Obadiah, walking his portion of the parched land in v.7, lifts his eyes and sees the prophet before him.
7:
Obadiah, walking his portion of the land, lifts his eyes and there stands the hunted prophet; and the witnesses read the meeting for the contrast between the two men's bearing. Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical voice, weighs the startled greeting with care. The words Art thou that my lord Elijah? may bear the sense the Septuagint gives — "Is it thy very self, my lord Elijah?" — or, "perhaps more suitably to the context," simply, "Thou here, my lord Elijah," when all the world is seeking thy life. Either way the astonishment is that the most wanted man in the kingdom should suddenly appear in the open field.

Ellicott's finest stroke is his reading of the reply. Against Obadiah's elaborate courtesy Elijah answers with two words: "The prophet's answer is still simpler in its original brevity, 'Behold, Elijah!' — standing in dignified contrast with the humble and almost servile address of Obadiah, which is clearly the offspring not only of reverence, but of fear." The steward bows and multiplies titles; the prophet, secure in his commission, needs only to name himself. In that asymmetry the grammatical-historical reading finds the whole difference between a good man who trembles in a wicked court and a prophet who fears no face of man.

The puritan Matthew Henry turns the salutation into a portrait of piety. Obadiah "saluted him with great respect, fell on his face, and humbly asked, Art thou that my lord Elijah?" — and "as he had shown the tenderness of a father to the sons of the prophets, so he showed the reverence of a son to this father of the prophets." By this, Henry says, Obadiah "made it appear that he did indeed fear God greatly, that he did honour to one that was his extraordinary ambassador and had a great interest in heaven." One may guess, Henry adds elsewhere, how people stand affected to God by observing how they stand affected to his people and ministers; and here the contrast is already sounding that will grow deafening when Ahab, in v.17, greets the same prophet not as "my lord" but as "the troubler of Israel." Reverence and railing meet the one man; the witnesses let the two greetings judge the two hearts.
8:
Elijah's answer — I am; go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here — is read by the witnesses as the openness of a man under orders and the humility of a prophet who refuses honour. John Gill catches the first note: Elijah "did not desire to be concealed; his orders were to show and make himself known to Ahab, and Obadiah was one of his domestic servants." The long hiding is over; the command of v.1 was to show himself, and now he does so deliberately, sending word ahead so that his appearing before the king shall be plainly his own act.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the delicacy of the message. Elijah "transfers the title of honour" that Obadiah had heaped on him and directs it to the king: "Call him thy lord, not me" — for that "is a fitter title for a prince than for a prophet, who seeks not honour from men." Henry adds the general rule: "Prophets should be called seers, and shepherds, and watchmen, and ministers, rather than lords, as those that mind duty more than dominion." And the instruction to carry word beforehand is itself considerate: Elijah "would have the king know before, that it might not be a surprise to him, and that he might be sure it was the prophet's own act to present himself to him." There is nothing furtive in the prophet; he walks into danger by the front door and announces it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the verse to open the scene of Obadiah's terror, which the next verses unfold. The plain command, go, tell thy lord, lands on a man who at once measures what it may cost him, and answers with the anguished question of v.9, "What have I sinned, that thou wouldest deliver thy servant into the hand of Ahab, to slay me?" So the verse stands at a hinge: on Elijah's side, calm obedience and self-forgetting readiness; on Obadiah's, the dread of a courtier who knows how murderous his master can be. The witnesses do not divide over it. They agree that the two words I am, and the errand that follows them, reveal a prophet wholly at God's disposal, seeking neither concealment for himself nor glory from men, and setting in motion the confrontation the whole chapter has been moving toward.
9:
Obadiah's stricken reply — What have I sinned, that thou wouldest deliver thy servant into the hand of Ahab, to slay me? — is read by the witnesses as the cry of a faithful man who suddenly sees his life set on a knife's edge. John Gill gives the sense directly: Obadiah asks "in what have I offended God or his prophet, that revenge should be taken on me in this way" — as though so deadly an errand could only be a punishment for some unknown fault. The steward cannot conceive why the prophet he reveres should hand him over to the king he dreads.

The force of the question lies in what Obadiah assumes the message will do to him, and the grammatical-historical witnesses supply it from the surrounding verses. To carry to Ahab the news Elijah is here is, in Obadiah's mind, to stake his own head on the prophet's remaining where he was found; and, as v.12 will show, he is sure the Spirit of the Lord will sweep Elijah away before the king can come, leaving the messenger to bear the fury of a disappointed tyrant. The single word sinned thus carries the whole weight of a good man's fear: he searches his conscience for a cause, finding none, and can only conclude that some hidden offence must have marked him out for such a peril.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the plea into his portrait of Obadiah's predicament. The steward "pleads that he did not deserve to be thus exposed, and put in peril of his life: 'What have I said amiss?'" It is not the protest of a coward but of a servant of God who has already risked much — who has hidden and fed a hundred prophets — and who cannot understand being treated, as he fears, "as one of the tools of Ahab's persecution." Henry's Obadiah is a man whose very fidelity makes the danger feel like a betrayal: he that had sheltered so many prophets hoped not to have his own life hazarded by so great a prophet. The witnesses do not contend here; together they hear in the question the trembling of a real and tender conscience, and they let the fuller apology of v.13 answer the wound that v.10 has already begun to explain.
10:
Obadiah's oath — As the Lord thy God liveth, there is no nation or kingdom whither my lord hath not sent to seek thee — is read by the witnesses as the key to the whole terror of his errand, and they divide gently over whose relentless search it really was. John Gill notes only that the form of an oath was chosen "to ascertain the truth of what he was about to say." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hear in the sweeping "to every kingdom and every nation" a hyperbole "suggested by inward excitement and fear," the language of a man whose dread magnifies the danger.

The interpretive weight, though, falls on what the search implies about the throne. Adam Clarke takes it at face value: Ahab "had sent through all his own states and to the neighbouring governments to find out the prophet," knowing from Elijah's own word that both rain and drought hung on his prayers; had he found him, he meant "to oblige him to procure rain, or punish him." The very extent of it, Clarke says, argues that "Ahab must have had considerable power and authority among the neighbouring nations to require and exact this, and Elijah must have kept himself very secret to have shunned such an extensive and minute search." Here is the outlier reading: the hunt is Ahab's own, the mark of a king with reach.

Against this stands Charles Ellicott, who finds the ferocity of the search out of character for the man he has drawn. "This unremitting search — implying perhaps some supremacy or authority over neighbouring kingdoms — suits ill with the half-hearted enmity of Ahab. No doubt it was the work of Jezebel, in Ahab's name, connived at (as in the murder of Naboth) by his timidity." For Ellicott the driving cruelty is always the queen's; Ahab merely lends his name and looks the other way, as he will do again over Naboth's vineyard. The debate is real but small: whether the earth-wide manhunt reveals Ahab's power (Clarke) or Jezebel's malice working through Ahab's weakness (Ellicott). On the substance the witnesses agree — the search was so total that Obadiah cannot believe he will escape it alive, and it is this that makes the message he is asked to carry, in v.11, feel to him like a sentence of death.
11:
The verse is little more than Obadiah repeating, in disbelief, the errand laid on him — And now thou sayest, Go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here — and the witnesses read it as the pivot of his fear rather than a fresh thought. John Gill supplies the unspoken dread: the message, "if I should not be able to make good, would be of fatal consequence to me; and that it is plain he feared, by what he next says." The steward turns the command over as a man turns over a thing that may kill him, and the very repetition betrays how impossible it seems.

The grammatical-historical reading sets the clause exactly where its force lies. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the Hebrew, join this verse to the oath before and the fear after: having sworn that Ahab's search had raked every kingdom, Obadiah now confronts the paradox that he, of all men, is asked to deliver the prophet up. The words and now thou sayest carry the whole weight of the contrast — after so long and so deadly a hunt, the quarry himself calmly bids his hunter's servant announce him. To Obadiah it is not deliverance but a trap: he cannot conceive that Elijah means to stay and face the king, and so the message seems designed to expose the messenger.

Because the verse is a hinge, the witnesses let it lean on what surrounds it. Behind it stands the sworn testimony of v.10, that no nation had been left unsearched; before it lies the reason Obadiah gives in v.12, that the Spirit of the Lord may snatch Elijah away the moment his back is turned. Held between the two, Behold, Elijah is here is for Obadiah a claim too dangerous to trust and too fatal to test. The prophet speaks with the composure of a man under orders from heaven; the steward hears the words of a man signing his death-warrant. The witnesses do not contend over so short a verse; together they read it as the exact moment when the deadly search of the past collides with the deadly errand of the present, and a good man's courage falters not from unbelief in God but from a very reasonable terror of Ahab.
12:
Obadiah names the precise thing he dreads — as soon as I am gone from thee, the Spirit of the Lord shall carry thee whither I know not — and the witnesses read the clause both for the fear it expresses and for the ancient idea of the Spirit it preserves. John Gill takes it as a natural supposition: Obadiah reckons that "small raptures might have been already, and known to Obadiah," so that Elijah could be swept off before the king arrived, leaving the messenger unable to point to him. Adam Clarke fills in the reasoning: Obadiah "supposed that the Spirit of the Lord had carried him to some strange country during the three years and a half of the drought," and, sure that God would not let his prophet fall into Ahab's murderous hands, "took for granted that as soon as he should come into danger, so soon would the Spirit of the Lord carry him away, or direct him to some hiding place."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in the phrase a survival of the oldest sense of the word: "In this phrase there is perhaps a survival of the original physical sense of the word 'Spirit' — the whirlwind which is 'the breath of the Lord.'" He points to the later catching-away of Elijah himself, when the sons of the prophets feared the Spirit had cast him on some mountain (2 Kings 2:16), and to the deacon Philip caught away by the Spirit of the Lord (Acts 8:39). To Obadiah, Ellicott says, "it seemed that only by such miraculous agency could Elijah have been removed from the persecution for so long a time, and that, having emerged for a moment, he will be swept away into his hidden refuge again."

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the same fear as a misjudgment of the prophet's intent. Obadiah "takes it for granted that Elijah would again withdraw," and even suspects "that Elijah was not in good earnest when he bade him tell Ahab where he was, but intended only to expose the impotency of Ahab's malice." Against all this the steward can only plead his own long fidelity, adding — as the ground of his hope for mercy — that he, "thy servant, fear the Lord from my youth." The witnesses agree: the fear is real, the theology of the rushing Spirit is genuine and old, and the whole speech is the earnest self-defence of a man who cannot yet believe that the prophet means to stand his ground before Ahab.
13:
Obadiah's last and strongest plea — Was it not told my lord what I did when Jezebel slew the prophets of the Lord? how I hid a hundred men by fifty in a cave, and fed them with bread and water? — is read by the witnesses as the argument of a man pleading not his merit but his loyalty. John Gill guards the motive with care: Obadiah recalls his deed "not in a way of ostentation, but to show that it would be very ungenerous and ungrateful, as well as impolitic, to sacrifice such a friend at court to the Lord's prophets as he had been, and might still continue to be." The point is not "I deserve a reward" but "surely you will not throw away one who has served your cause at the risk of his life."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the plea in its narrative frame. Obadiah rehearses the persecution — Jezebel cutting off the prophets of the Lord — and his own secret counter-work, hiding and feeding a hundred of them, precisely to establish that though he wears Ahab's livery he is not of Ahab's party. His fear, they note, is that the errand will cost him his life; and it is against that fear that the appeal to his record is set. Adam Clarke adds the historical setting, dating "this persecution probably during the dearth," when Jezebel, unable to find Elijah, "would naturally wreak her vengeance on the prophets of Jehovah who were within her reach." The very famine that Elijah's word had brought became the pretext for the queen's fury, and Obadiah's caves were the answer to it.

The puritan Matthew Henry hears the same note of self-vindication without vanity. Obadiah mentions the hidden hundred "to convince Elijah that though he was Ahab's servant he was not in his interest, and therefore deserved not to be bantered as one of the tools of his persecution." He who "had protected so many prophets, he hoped, should not have his own life hazarded by so great a prophet." The witnesses do not divide; together they read the verse as the moving climax of Obadiah's fear — a faithful courtier laying his one great service before the prophet, less as a claim on gratitude than as a proof that he is a friend and not a snare. It is to this whole anxious speech, gathered from v.9 onward, that Elijah's steadying oath in v.15 is the calm and sufficient reply.
14:
The verse repeats, almost word for word, the fear Obadiah has voiced throughout — And now thou sayest, Go, tell thy lord, Behold, Elijah is here: and he shall slay me — and the witnesses read the repetition itself as the mark of a terror that cannot let the matter rest. John Gill states the ground plainly: Obadiah dreads that "should he carry such a message to Ahab, and Elijah should be removed elsewhere, and not to be found," the king's rage would fall on the messenger. The whole speech circles back to its beginning, because for Obadiah the danger has not changed: to announce the prophet is to guarantee his own death if the prophet vanishes.

The grammatical-historical reading treats the verse as the closing beat of a single anxious argument. Keil and Delitzsch, following the Hebrew through vv.13–14, show how the plea and the fear are bound together: having urged his loyalty in hiding the hundred prophets, Obadiah returns at once to the one dread that dominates him — and he shall slay me. The bluntness of the ending is deliberate; stripped of all the earlier reasoning about the Spirit and the search and his own record, the fear reduces at last to three words. Obadiah has said everything he can say, and it all comes down to the naked expectation of being killed.

Because the verse adds no new thought, the witnesses let it stand as the emotional low point from which Elijah's answer will lift the scene. The puritan Matthew Henry, gathering the passage, reads the repeated he shall slay me as the voice of a man who has convinced himself that Ahab, disappointed of his prey, would be "unreasonably outrageous" even toward a faithful servant — for "tyrants and persecutors, in their passion, are often unreasonably outrageous, even towards their friends and confidants." The steward's fear is thus not cowardice but a shrewd reading of his master's temper. And it is precisely this settled dread — that the errand means death — which makes the prophet's sworn assurance in the next verse so necessary. The witnesses do not contend over the verse; they hear in it the last, plain, frightened word of Obadiah before Elijah, in v.15, puts the whole fear to rest with an oath.
15:
Elijah answers all of Obadiah's fear with a single oath — As the Lord of hosts liveth, before whom I stand, I will surely show myself unto him to day — and the witnesses read it as the word that turns dread into obedience. John Gill marks its purpose and its tenderness: the prophet "takes this oath, to assure Obadiah that he would certainly be upon the spot, or to be found, and not expose him to any danger." What the steward feared above all was that Elijah would vanish and leave him to Ahab's fury; the oath meets that fear exactly, pledging that this very day the prophet will stand before the king in person.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the solemn title Elijah swears by — the Lord of hosts, before whom I stand — and note that on this assurance "Obadiah went to announce it to the king," his terror at once dissolved. The form of the oath is itself an argument: Elijah swears by Jehovah Sabaoth, the Lord of the armies of heaven, in whose presence he ministers as a servant on duty. To stand before the Lord of hosts is to be under orders that no earthly king can overrule, and the prophet stakes that relation on his promise.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the comfort hidden in the divine name. Elijah "swears by the Lord of hosts, who has all power in his hands, and is therefore able to protect his servants against all the powers of hell and earth." The very title that makes the oath binding also makes it safe: the God of hosts who commands the prophet can shield the messenger. Once "Obadiah knew that he spoke seriously, and really intended it, he will make no scruple to carry the message to Ahab." So the long scene of the steward's fear, running from v.9, is closed by one sentence. The witnesses do not divide; together they read the oath as the prophet's calm self-committal — a man who stands before the Lord of hosts has nothing to fear from Ahab, and can promise, on the life of God, to walk openly into the king's presence before the day is out. Upon that word Obadiah goes, and the meeting of prophet and king in v.16 follows at once.
16:
The messenger's fear having been laid to rest, the verse moves swiftly — So Obadiah went to meet Ahab, and told him: and Ahab went to meet Elijah — and the witnesses read it as the quiet threshold of the great confrontation. John Gill notes that Obadiah acted "upon the prophet's oath," being now "entirely satisfied, and in no fear of delivering the message"; the sworn word of v.15 has done its work, and the steward carries the news that Elijah is ready to appear before the king wherever he pleases.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch let the verse stand as the narrator's hinge into the drama of Carmel: Obadiah tells the king, and Ahab, long baffled in his search, at once goes out to meet the man he could never find. The very promptness is telling — the king who had sent to every nation now walks out to a meeting arranged by the prophet himself.

The puritan Matthew Henry dwells on the strangeness of the reversal and the state of Ahab's heart. "We may suppose it was a great surprise to Ahab to hear that Elijah, whom he had so long sought and not found, was now found without seeking. He went in quest of grass, and found him from whose word, at God's mouth, he must expect rain." Yet the king's conscience "gave him little reason to hope for it, but, rather, to fear some other more dreadful judgment." Henry reads the whole psychology of the encounter before a word is spoken: "Had he, by his spies, surprised Elijah, he would have triumphed over him; but, now that he was thus surprised by him, we may suppose he even trembled to look him in the face, hated him, and yet feared him, as Herod did John." The image is exact — a guilty king drawn against his will to the prophet he loathes, unable either to strike or to submit. The witnesses do not divide over so plain a verse; together they read it as the moment the two long-separated antagonists at last come face to face, the hunted prophet composed and obedient, the hunting king surprised, uneasy, and afraid, the stage now set for the accusation of v.17 and the reckoning at Carmel.
17:
The two great antagonists meet, and Ahab strikes first: Art thou he that troubleth Israel? The witnesses read the greeting as self-revelation. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fasten on the Hebrew ocher, "troubler," the word used of Achan who brought disaster on the camp (Genesis 34:30), and note how Elijah throws the charge straight back: "It is not I who have brought Israel into trouble, but thou and thy family." Charles Ellicott would render the taunt more sharply still — "Thou here, the troubler of Israel!" — a man "defying vengeance in the very land which thou hast troubled."

The puritan Matthew Henry makes the verse a study in how men betray themselves by their words. Ahab "durst not strike him, remembering that Jeroboam's hand withered when it was stretched out against a prophet, but gave him bad language, which was no less an affront to Him that sent him." Henry sets the two greetings side by side: "How unlike was this to that with which his servant Obadiah saluted him — Art thou that my lord Elijah?" And he draws the rule that governs the whole chapter: "One may guess how people stand affected to God by observing how they stand affected to his people and ministers." The same prophet who is "my lord" to a God-fearing steward is "thou troubler" to a God-forsaking king; the two hearts are read off the two salutations.

Henry presses the cruel irony of the charge. Elijah "now came to bring blessings to Israel, tidings of the return of the rain; yet he was thus affronted." Far from being the nation's enemy, he was "the stay of it, the chariots and horsemen of Israel." And Henry lifts the moment into a law of the servants of God: "It has been the lot of the best and most useful men to be called and counted the troublers of the land, and to be run down as public grievances. Even Christ and his apostles were thus misrepresented" (Acts 17:6). Had the accusation been true, Henry grants, a king would have been bound to act on it; but it was utterly false, and the falseness is the point. The witnesses do not divide; together they hear in Ahab's opening insult the whole disposition of a heart that hates the messenger because it will not face the message, and they wait for Elijah's counter-charge in v.18 to name the real troubler of Israel.
18:
Elijah flings the accusation back with proof: I have not troubled Israel; but thou, and thy father's house, in that ye have forsaken the commandments of the Lord, and thou hast followed Baalim. The witnesses read it as the exact placing of blame that the whole drought was meant to teach. Adam Clarke states the doctrine plainly: "Here the cause of the dearth is placed on its true ground: the king and the people had forsaken the true God, and God shut up the heavens that there was no rain. Elijah was only the minister whom God used to dispense this judgment." The prophet is the messenger of the trouble, never its author; the sin that brought it lay in the palace.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott attends to the plural Baalim. It is, he says, "as usual, 'the Baalim' — the phrase being probably used contemptuously for false gods generally," the Baal and the Asherah "and perhaps other Canaanitish idols being included." The single apostasy of the court had multiplied into a whole pantheon, and Elijah names them all with one scornful plural. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the retort in the Hebrew, likewise set thou and thy father's house against Ahab's thou: the guilt is dynastic, the accumulated idolatry of Omri's line, and it is this that has drawn down the shut heaven.

The puritan Matthew Henry drives the reversal home with the figure Ahab had reached for. "Elijah is not the Achan: 'I have not troubled Israel, have neither done them any wrong nor designed them any hurt.'" On the contrary, "those that procure God's judgments do the mischief, not he that merely foretells them and gives warning of them, that the nation may repent and prevent them." The prophet would have healed Israel; it was the people who would not be healed. "Ahab is the Achan, the troubler, who follows Baalim, those accursed things." And Henry lifts it into a maxim for every age: "Nothing creates more trouble to a land than the impiety and profaneness of princes and their families." The witnesses do not divide; together they make the verse the moral center of the meeting — the drought is no arbitrary cruelty and the prophet no public enemy, but the sin of the throne is the true troubler of Israel, and the only cure is the return to the forsaken commandments that the coming trial at Carmel will force the nation to choose.
19:
Elijah's summons — gather to me all Israel unto mount Carmel, and the prophets of Baal four hundred and fifty, and the prophets of the groves four hundred, which eat at Jezebel's table — draws the witnesses to the ground, the numbers, and the strategy. Charles Ellicott, the fullest here, expounds the mountain. Carmel means "a garden" or "park," and the ridge, "well planted and watered, of central Palestine," runs some twelve miles from the hills of Manasseh nearly to the sea. Near its eastern height stands a place still called El Maharrakah, "the burning," in view of Jezreel and commanding a glimpse of the sea — "the traditional (and highly probable) scene of Elijah's sacrifice." Carmel had fallen to Asher (Joshua 19:26) and was proverbial for its beauty (Isaiah 33:9; Song of Solomon 7:6); the presence of an old altar of the Lord shows it was long a high place. "No more striking scene could well be found for the great drama of this chapter."

The wesleyan Adam Clarke does the arithmetic of idolatry. King and queen "had different religious establishments": Ahab and his servants worshipped Baal — "the supreme lord and master of the world, the sun" — with four hundred and fifty priests; the queen and her women worshipped Asherah, "Astarte, or Venus," with four hundred more, who "ate at Jezebel's table" and "made a part of her household." These, Clarke reckons, were the domestic chaplains of the court, not the whole heathen priesthood of the land. Both Clarke and Ellicott mark a curiosity that runs through the chapter: though summoned, the four hundred prophets of the groves "do not appear at all in the subsequent scene."

The reformed John Gill reads the choice of Carmel as prophetic strategy. Elijah "desired that all Israel might be convened, that it might be openly and publicly done, and to the conviction and reformation of them, which was what was chiefly designed." He chose Carmel rather than Samaria "that he might meet with no obstruction from Jezebel," and because from its height "he might be able to see the rain when coming, as he did." The witnesses do not divide; together they set the stage — a natural amphitheatre famous for the beauty a three-year drought had scorched, the massed priesthoods of a royal idolatry on one side, one prophet on the other, and all Israel gathered as jury. Everything is arranged for a public and decisive trial, which Ahab, in v.20, obediently convenes.
20:
The half-verse records only that Ahab sent unto all the children of Israel, and gathered the prophets together unto mount Carmel, yet the witnesses find in the king's compliance a revealing thing. John Gill notes the practical machinery — Ahab sent "by messengers, requiring their attendance at Mount Carmel at such a time, at least their chief and principal men" — the summons reaching the heads and rulers of the tribes if not every private Israelite. That the king should obey the prophet he has just called "the troubler of Israel" is itself remarkable.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs why so proud a persecutor should submit to be Elijah's herald. He offers two motives. Either Ahab "feared Elijah, and durst not oppose him" — as "Saul stood in awe of Samuel more than of God" — "or because he hoped Elijah would bless the land, and speak the word that they might have rain, and upon those terms they would be all at his beck." Self-interest and superstition together bend the king to the prophet's will; those who slighted Elijah's counsels "would gladly be beholden to him for his prayers." The convening of the assembly is thus no repentance, only the cringing of a man who wants his rain and fears his God's messenger.

Henry lifts the moment into a larger providence. In gathering the idolatrous nation to Carmel, God "made those who said they were Jews and were not, but were of the synagogue of Satan, to come, and, in effect, to worship at his feet, and to know that God had loved him" (Revelation 3:9). The apostate people are summoned by their own apostate king to the very ground where their idolatry will be exposed and their God vindicated. The witnesses do not contend over so brief a verse; together they read Ahab's obedience as the reluctant instrument of a divine design, the king unwittingly assembling the witnesses of his own idols' defeat. What he intends as the price of rain becomes the occasion of the reckoning, and the whole nation is now in place for the searching question Elijah will put to them in v.21. The drama that the geography of Carmel was made for can now begin, with all Israel gathered to see it.
21:
Elijah opens the trial not with the sacrifice but with a question that pierces the nation's heart: How long halt ye between two opinions? If the Lord be God, follow him: but if Baal, then follow him. The witnesses savour the image and the demand. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch translate the Hebrew, "How long do ye limp upon both sides?" — the people wanting "to combine the worship of Jehovah and Baal," and so struck silent, "since they knew very well from the law itself that Jehovah demanded worship with a whole and undivided heart" (Deuteronomy 6:4-5; compare the divided heart of Psalms 119:113). Adam Clarke renders it more vividly still: "How long hop ye about upon two boughs?" — like a bird that cannot settle — and adds the note of lameness: "they dreaded Jehovah, and therefore could not totally abandon him; they feared the king and queen, and therefore thought they must embrace the religion of the state."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott calls the question "the very motto of Elijah's life." The halting between two opinions was "at all times more dangerous, because more easy, than open apostasy," suiting "the accommodating genius of such polytheism" but "utterly incompatible with the sole absolute claim of the worship of Jehovah." He hears behind it the indignant expostulation of Ezekiel (Ezekiel 20:39), and observes that "the question, once clearly understood, is always unanswerable, and is listened to here in awestruck silence."

The puritan Matthew Henry expounds the sin most fully. The people "worshipped God to please the prophets, but worshipped Baal to please Jezebel and curry favour at court"; they "thought to trim the matter, and play on both sides." Elijah waives all appeal to Israel's ancient relation to Jehovah and puts it on the naked merits: there can be but one God, and if Baal be he, cleave to him; but if Jehovah, "Baal is a cheat, and you must have no more to do with him." Henry cites bishop Hall — in "necessary hostility as betwixt God and Baal, he that is not with God is against him" — and sets the choice beside Joshua's, choose you this day whom ye will serve (Joshua 24:15). To it all "they answered him not a word," unable to justify themselves and unwilling to condemn themselves. The witnesses are of one mind: divided worship is no worship, and the silence of Israel is the first confession that Elijah's question has already won.
22:
Elijah states the odds — I, even I only, remain a prophet of the Lord; but Baal's prophets are four hundred and fifty men — and the witnesses read the loneliness both as fact and as strategy. John Gill softens the claim to what the prophet could see: he was "the only prophet of the Lord" present, "at least as he thought, all the rest being slain, as he supposed; however there were none present but himself." The hundred hidden in Obadiah's caves do not contradict the boast; on the platform of Carmel, Elijah truly stands alone.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke reads the words as a statement of visible disproportion: "I am the only prophet of God present, and can have but the influence of an individual; while the prophets of Baal are four hundred and fifty men." Clarke also solves the arithmetic that puzzles readers of v.19: "the queen's prophets, amounting to four hundred, were not at this great assembly," which is why only the four hundred and fifty of Baal appear here — the same court prophets Ahab will consult before Ramoth-gilead (1 Kings 22:6). The four hundred of the groves stay at Jezebel's table and out of the fire.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch see in the very inequality the fairness of the coming test. Because "the people adhered to their undecided double-mindedness," Elijah "proposed to let the Deity Himself decide who was the true God, Jehovah or Baal." Each side would offer a sacrifice, and "the true God should make Himself known by kindling the burnt-offering presented to Him with fire from heaven." The proposal, they note, rests on the precedent of Leviticus 9:24, where fire from the Lord consumed the first sacrifice at the tabernacle; Elijah hopes the Lord will so reveal himself again. And "the form of decision thus proposed would necessarily appear all the fairer, because Elijah, the prophet of Jehovah, stood alone in opposition to a whole crowd of Baal's prophets, numbering no less than four hundred and fifty men." The witnesses do not divide; together they turn the prophet's solitude into the trial's guarantee of honesty — one man against four hundred and fifty, so that if fire falls it can be credited to no numbers, no conspiracy, no priestly craft, but only to the God who answers.
23:
Elijah sets the terms of the ordeal — let them give us two bullocks; let them choose one, cut it in pieces, lay it on wood, and put no fire under; and I will dress the other, and put no fire under — and the witnesses read the proposal for its scrupulous fairness. John Gill notes that the prophets of Baal, "being so many, were better able to be at the expense of them, and having the king on their party too," though perhaps no more is meant than that two bullocks "should be brought thither, and presented before them." The challenger asks nothing for himself that he does not grant to his opponents.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the whole structure of the test as the verse frames it. Each party dresses its victim and lays it on the wood; then "call ye on the name of your gods, and I will call on the name of the Lord: and the God that answereth by fire, let him be God." The condition put no fire under, repeated of both altars, is the heart of the arrangement — nothing is to be kindled by human hand, so that whatever flame appears must fall from heaven. The prophets of Baal, being many, are even granted the first attempt.

The design of the verse is a study in equity, and the witnesses draw it out from what surrounds it. Elijah yields every visible advantage — the numbers, the royal favour, the choice of bullock, the precedence of the trial — and keeps for himself only the bare truth of his cause. The test is so constructed that fraud is impossible and partiality unthinkable: two sacrifices, identically prepared, neither touched with fire, and the verdict left wholly to the God who can answer. By insisting that no fire be laid beneath, Elijah forecloses in advance the very trick that heathen altars were known for, and stakes the whole issue on a miracle that no man can counterfeit. The witnesses do not contend over the arrangement; together they admire its openness, and see in it the confidence of a prophet who, precisely because his God is the living God, can afford to give the idol every chance. The people's ready assent — it is well spoken — which the next verse records, is the natural response to terms so manifestly just, and it commits the whole assembly to abide by the fire of v.24.
24:
The test is sealed by its condition — the God that answereth by fire, let him be God — and the people close with it: It is well spoken. The witnesses probe why fire, of all signs, was chosen. The wesleyan Adam Clarke finds a pointed shrewdness in it: since "the Baal who was worshipped here was incontestably Apollo, or the sun," he was "the god of fire, and had only to work in his own element." Elijah thus "gave them every advantage," staking the contest on the very sphere the idol was supposed to rule.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the sign in its biblical company. This "gift of a sign from heaven" was "not unfamiliar to Israelite experience" (Leviticus 9:24; 1 Chronicles 21:26; 2 Chronicles 7:1). Such a sign, our Lord teaches, "may not be craved for or demanded as a ground of faith" (Matthew 12:38-39), yet "like all other miracles" it is "granted unasked when it is seen by God's wisdom to be needed, in order to startle an ignorant and misguided people into serious attention." And because "the worship of Baal was a worship of the power of Nature, impersonated perhaps in the sun," the miracle "entered on the visible sphere, especially usurped in his name, in order to claim it for the Lord Jehovah."

The puritan Matthew Henry finds a deeper fitness in fire rather than water. Though rain was the thing the country needed, Elijah asks for fire, "because the atonement was to be made by sacrifice, before the judgment could be removed in mercy. The God therefore that has power to pardon sin, and to signify it by consuming the sin-offering, must needs be the God that can relieve us against the calamity." In a word, "He that can give fire can give rain." The people, Henry adds, "allow the proposal to be fair and unexceptionable": God had often answered by fire, and "if Baal cannot do so, let him be cast out for a usurper." Those firm for God doubted not the issue; the wavering were willing to be determined; and neither Ahab nor the priests dared object for fear of the crowd. The witnesses agree: the terms are just, the sign is aimed at the idol's own boast, and all Israel has now bound itself to the verdict of the fire.
25:
The trial begins, and Elijah, with deliberate courtesy, gives Baal's prophets the first turn: Choose you one bullock for yourselves, and dress it first; for ye are many; and call on the name of your gods, but put no fire under. The witnesses read the concession as both fair and shrewd. Adam Clarke takes the reason at face value — "for ye are many; and therefore shall have the preference, and the advantage of being first in your application to the deity." The greater number is given the greater opportunity, that no complaint of unfair haste may afterward be raised.

The reformed John Gill probes why the priests of Baal accepted terms so dangerous to themselves, gathering the guesses of the older expositors. Ben Gersom thought they agreed "because that, according to their belief, Baal was Mars, and in the sign of Aries, one of the fiery planets," and so imagined he could send down fire; Abarbinel held it was "the sun they worshipped, under the name of Baal, the great luminary which presides over the element of fire," and therefore had power to make it descend. Failing such confidence, Gill offers three plainer motives: necessity, since after the people had approved the test they dared not refuse "lest they should rise upon them, and stone them"; the hope that Elijah, offering away from the temple "contrary to the law of his God," would get no answer either, leaving both sides even; and the expectation that, sacrificing together, any fire that fell might serve them all.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads Elijah's yielding of precedence as calculated for their confusion. He "gives them the lead for their greater confusion; only, knowing that the working of Satan is with lying wonders, he takes care to prevent a fraud: Be sure to put no fire under." The one safeguard the prophet insists on is the very thing that will make Baal's failure undeniable — no concealed flame, no priestly sleight, nothing but the naked appeal to a god who cannot answer. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in this verse the calm confidence of Elijah, who can afford to let the idol go first and try its utmost, precisely because he knows the outcome, and who allows his adversaries every advantage save the one chance to cheat. The long, fruitless clamour of v.26 follows at once.
26:
The prophets of Baal take their bullock and cry from morning until noon — O Baal, hear us — leaping about the altar, and the witnesses read the scene as the exposure of idolatry by its own frenzy. Adam Clarke lays out the shape of the day: the priests "employed the whole day in their desperate rites," the first period "from morning until noon" spent "in preparing and offering the sacrifice, and in earnest supplication for the celestial fire." The leaping he corrects toward the margin — "they leaped up and down at the altar; they danced round it with strange and hideous cries and gesticulations," a spectacle he compares to the contortions of the heathen priests he had himself watched at their offerings.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the wild cry against the coming stillness of Elijah's prayer. "This repeated cry — the ever-recurring burden of the prayer, uttered probably first in measured chant, afterwards in a wild excited cry — stands in an instructive contrast with the simple, earnest solemnity of the prayer of Elijah." He hears in it the very fault our Lord condemned in the heathen, "who think that they shall be heard for their much speaking" (Matthew 6:7), and points to "a grave irony in the notice of the blank silence which followed this frenzied cry: There was no voice, nor any to answer, nor any that regarded."

The puritan Matthew Henry pictures the noise and its futility. We may imagine, he says, "what a noise four hundred and fifty men made, when they cried as one man, and with all their might, O Baal! hear us," and this "for some hours together, longer than Diana's worshippers made their cry, Great is Diana of the Ephesians" (Acts 19:34). "How senseless, how brutish, were they in their addresses to Baal!" — leaping "like fools" upon the altar "as if they would themselves become sacrifices with their bullock." The witnesses do not divide; together they let the long, loud, unanswered morning make its own argument. The more the priests shout and leap, the plainer it grows that no one is listening; the emptiness of the heavens over Baal is measured out in every hour of clamour, and the silence itself is the first verdict, which Elijah's noon mockery in v.27 will turn into open scorn.
27:
At noon Elijah begins to mock — Cry aloud, for he is a god; either he is talking, or he is pursuing, or he is on a journey, or peradventure he sleepeth, and must be awaked — and the witnesses divide only over how much fine detail to press from the taunt. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch locate the sting in the refrain "for he is a god," set ironically against the list of all-too-human excuses, and heightened by the threefold repetition; with Clericus they observe that such absurdities, spoken of God, "idolaters could believe, as we may see from Homer." Adam Clarke, by contrast, delights in unpacking each clause — Baal may be in profound revery, or "taking his pleasure in hunting," or gone on some excursion, and, citing Rabbi Jarchi, even reaches the coarsest reading of "he sleepeth," noting that "among Asiatic idolaters their gods have different functions to fulfill, and require sleep and rest," as Vishnu sleeps four months in the year.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott checks that ingenuity. The mockery, he says, "has been with over-ingenuity explained as applying to various supposed actions of Baal. It is merely the bitter irony of sheer contempt, calling Baal a god only to heap upon him ideas most ungodlike." He sets it in noble company — "not unlike the righteous scorn of the psalmists or the prophets" for the idols (Psalms 115:4-8; Isaiah 44:9-20) — yet marks the difference from Paul's courtesy at Athens (Acts 17:22-23): "There was no place for toleration" here, for "the case was not of heathen ignorance, but of Israel's apostasy."

The puritan Matthew Henry justifies the ridicule and guards it at once. "The worship of idols is a most ridiculous thing, and it is but justice to represent it so and expose it to scorn." Yet, he warns, "this will by no means justify those who ridicule the worshippers of God in Christ because the worship is not performed just in their way." Henry notes, too, that the mockery only inflamed the priests: "so far were they from being convinced and put to shame by the just reproach Elijah cast upon them, that it made them the more violent." The witnesses agree on the substance — the taunt is not cruelty but the truth of idolatry spoken plainly — and differ only in temper, Clarke expanding every barb, Ellicott paring them back to one contemptuous point, while the frenzied self-wounding of v.28 answers the scorn.
28:
The frenzy deepens into blood: they cried aloud, and cut themselves after their manner with knives and lancets, till the blood gushed out upon them. The witnesses read the self-wounding as the native cruelty of false worship. Adam Clarke traces the rite across the pagan world: "this was done according to the rites of that barbarous religion; if the blood of the bullock would not move him they thought their own blood might; and with it they smeared themselves and their sacrifice." Nor was it peculiar to Baal — it "was not only the custom of the idolatrous Israelites, but of the Syrians, Persians, Greeks, Indians, and in short of all the heathen world."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the translation and probes the impulse. "Lancets should be lances," he notes; and the self-mutilation, "common in Oriental frenzy, was possibly a portion, or a survival, of human sacrifice," resting on "the notion that self-torture and shedding of human blood must win Divine favour — a delusion not confined to heathen religions, though excusable only in them." The gashing is thus no mere excess but a theology in miniature: a god who must be bought with pain, and whose silence only drives his votaries to bleed the more.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the contrast with the God of Israel that the whole scene is teaching. The priests cut themselves "in a sort of prophetic fury, hoping to obtain the favour of their god by offering to him their own blood, when they could not obtain it with the blood of their bullock. God never required his worshippers thus to honour him." Henry sets against it the express prohibition of the law — Ye shall not cut yourselves (Deuteronomy 14:1) — and the searching principle that the Lord "insists upon it that we mortify our lusts and corruptions," but that "corporeal penances and severities, which have no tendency to that, are no pleasure to him. Who has required these things at your hands?" The service of the devil, he observes, "though in some instances it pleases and pampers the body, yet in other things it is really cruel to it." The witnesses do not divide; together they let the gashed and bleeding priests stand as the visible sign of a religion that takes everything and answers nothing, and they mark that even this last extremity of zeal — poured-out blood on a screaming altar — moves the heavens not at all, as the flat silence of v.29 declares.
29:
The long day burns out to nothing: when midday was past, they prophesied until the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice; and there was neither voice, nor any to answer, nor any that regarded. The witnesses fasten on the odd word prophesied and on the total silence that answers it. Adam Clarke presses the philology: "they made incessant prayer and supplication; a farther proof that to pray or supplicate is the proper ideal meaning of the word naba, which we constantly translate to prophesy," even where every circumstance is against such a sense — as when Saul was found "among the prophets" (1 Samuel 10:5). The priests do not foretell; they rave in petition.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sharpens the picture into a study of true and false inspiration. They "raved in their frenzy, like Saul in the hour of madness" (1 Samuel 18:10; 1 Samuel 19:20-24). And here he draws the line that the whole contest is meant to teach: "As a rule, the true prophetic inspiration, even if felt as overmastering the will, gave no place to frenzy. The spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets" (1 Corinthians 14:32). Baal's ecstasy is loss of self-command; the Spirit of God, even at its most constraining, leaves a man master of himself.

The puritan Matthew Henry contemplates the deaf heavens above the din. Some of the priests prayed while others "prophesied, sang hymns," or encouraged the rest that Baal would answer at last; "but there was no answer, nor any that regarded. Idols could do neither good nor evil." Henry adds a striking theological aside: the prince of the power of the air, "if God had permitted him, could have caused fire to come down from heaven on this occasion, and gladly would have done it for the support of his Baal" — for the beast of the Apocalypse "maketh fire come down from heaven in the sight of men, and so deceiveth them" (Revelation 13:13-14). "But God would not suffer the devil to do it now, because the trial of his title was put on that issue by consent of parties." The witnesses agree: after morning cries, noon mockery, and afternoon blood, the verdict of the heavens over Baal is one unbroken silence, and it is into that silence that Elijah steps to build his altar in v.30.
30:
The idol's day being spent, Elijah calls the people close and begins his own work: Come near unto me... And he repaired the altar of the Lord that was broken down. The witnesses read the ruined and restored altar as a deliberate act of reformation. John Gill and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that there was "already an altar of Jehovah upon Carmel," dating either from the days before the temple, when altars stood throughout the land, or built by "pious worshippers" of the north; Elijah gathers the people near "that he might have both eye-witnesses and ear-witnesses present at his sacrifice."

The wesleyan Adam Clarke enriches the note with the mountain's long fame. There had been "an altar of Jehovah in that place, called, even among the heathens, the altar of Carmel." He cites Tacitus and Suetonius, who record that Vespasian went up to consult the oracle of Carmel, where "there was no temple nor statue, but simply an altar that was respectable for its antiquity," and where a priest named Basilides assured him of success. "The mount, the absence of a temple, no image, but a simple altar, very ancient," Clarke argues, "prove that this was originally the altar of Jehovah," though by Roman times overrun with "lying vanities."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the broken altar as a sign of the times: it is "evidently referred to as well known, and here accepted by Elijah as having a true sacredness," for "the exclusive consecration of the appointed sanctuary at Jerusalem" was "now obviously broken down by the religious severance of Israel." The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the theology of the repair. Elijah "would not make use of theirs, which had been polluted with their prayers to Baal, but, finding the ruins of an altar there, which had formerly been used in the service of the Lord, he chose to repair that" — to show "that he was not about to introduce any new religion, but to revive the faith and worship of their fathers' God, and reduce them to their first love, their first works." Where reformation could not go the whole way — he could not bring divided Israel back to Jerusalem — he did what he could. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in the mended altar a parable of the prophet's whole errand: not innovation but restoration, the rebuilding of a broken covenant on the very stones an apostate generation had let fall, which he now numbers by the tribes in v.31.
31:
Elijah takes his stones by number and by name: twelve stones, according to the number of the tribes of the sons of Jacob, unto whom the word of the Lord came, saying, Israel shall be thy name. The witnesses read the twelve as a silent sermon on the unity of a divided nation. Keil and Delitzsch, the fullest here, call the twelve stones "a practical declaration on the part of the prophet that the division of the nation into two kingdoms was at variance with the divine calling of Israel," since "according to the will of God the twelve tribes were to form one people of Jehovah, and to have a common sacrificial altar." The reminder that God had named the patriarch Israel (Genesis 32:29; Genesis 35:10) "directs attention to the wrong which the seceding ten tribes had done in claiming the name of Israel for themselves," when it belonged to the whole covenant people.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott states the same truth as consolation rather than rebuke: "The emphatic notice of these, as emblematic of the twelve tribes, is significant. In spite of political division, and even religious separation, the tribes were still united in the covenant of God." The stones testify that the breach of Jeroboam and the apostasy of Ahab have not dissolved the ancient oneness in the eye of God.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out both the reproach and the encouragement. Though ten of the tribes "had revolted to Baal, he would look upon them as belonging to God still, by virtue of the ancient covenant with their fathers"; and though divided from the other two "in civil interest, yet in the worship of the God of Israel they had communion with each other, and they twelve were one." Henry hears in the recollection of the new name a double purpose: it shames "his degenerate seed, who worshipped a god which they saw could not hear nor answer them," and it heartens the prophet who "was now to wrestle with God as Jacob did" — for he too "shall be a prince with God" (compare Hosea 12:4). The witnesses do not divide; together they find in the twelve stones a quiet, weighty claim — that beneath all the fractures of kingdom and cult, Israel is one people bound to one God, and that the altar now rising on Carmel is built not for the ten tribes only but for the whole household of Jacob, upon whom the coming fire will fall.
32:
The stones become an altar, and the altar is ringed with a trench: with the stones he built an altar in the name of the Lord: and he made a trench about the altar, as great as would contain two measures of seed. The witnesses read the building and the trench for their meaning and their measurement. John Gill keeps the theology of the twelve stones in view: they whom "the twelve tribes had formerly worshipped," though "now divided in their civil state, yet ought to be united in the worship of God." The altar is raised deliberately in the name of the Lord — by his authority and for his glory, as Keil and Delitzsch note the Hebrew requires.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke explains the purpose of the surrounding channel: the trench "was to detain the water that might fall down from the altar when the barrels should be poured upon it," so that the coming flood should not simply drain away but stand as witness. The grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs the odd measurement — a trench "as great as would contain two measures of seed." The measure, he explains, "the third part of the ephah," was "something less than three gallons"; but "a trench to contain only six gallons seems too insignificant for the context," and so he prefers the sense that it was "large enough for the sowing of two measures of seed," a considerable compass laid out around the altar.

The verse, plain as it is, prepares the great precaution that follows, and the witnesses draw the point from its setting. Elijah is not merely building; he is building for a public and unanswerable proof. The altar in the name of the Lord declares whose contest this is; the wide trench, dug before a single barrel is poured, shows that the drenching to come was planned in advance, part of a deliberate design to make the miracle beyond suspicion. Nothing here is improvised. The prophet who gave Baal's priests the whole day and every advantage now takes pains, even in the shape of a ditch, to ensure that when the fire falls no man can whisper of concealed flame or lucky spark. The witnesses do not contend over the verse; together they see in the altar and its trench the careful stagecraft of faith, which fears no scrutiny and therefore invites it, and which arranges the very ground so that the answer by fire in v.38 shall admit of only one explanation.
33:
Elijah lays the sacrifice and then calls for water: he put the wood in order, and cut the bullock in pieces, and laid him on the wood, and said, Fill four barrels with water, and pour it on the burnt sacrifice, and on the wood. The witnesses read the drenching as the masterstroke against all suspicion of fraud. Adam Clarke explains it fully: the water "was done to prevent any kind of suspicion that there was fire concealed under the altar." He cites an ancient writer who had seen beneath heathen altars "holes dug in the earth with funnels," by which hidden priests could set the wood ablaze and persuade "the simple people that the sacrifice was consumed by a miraculous fire." Elijah forecloses every such trick: "Had there been a concealed fire under the altar, the water that was thrown on the altar must have extinguished it most effectually. This very precaution has for ever put this miracle beyond the reach of suspicion."

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the bare preparations into faith and type. "Behold the bullock and the wood; but where is the fire?" — and he answers with Abraham on Moriah, God will provide himself a lamb (Genesis 22:7-8): "God will provide himself fire. If we, in sincerity, offer our hearts to God, he will, by his grace, kindle a holy fire in them." Henry marvels that the sacrifice was accepted at all, for "Elijah was no priest, nor were his attendants Levites," and "Carmel had neither tabernacle nor temple," being far from the ark and the chosen place; "yet never was any sacrifice more acceptable to God than this." The very setting aside of the Levitical rule — as often in the days of the judges and of Samuel — he reads as a hint that those institutions "were more designed for types to be fulfilled in the evangelical anti-types than for laws to be fulfilled in the strict observance of them," their passing away foretold (Colossians 2:22; Hebrews 8:13).

The witnesses do not divide over the verse; together they see in the ordered wood, the dismembered bullock, and the first drenching a sacrifice at once scrupulously honest and richly typical — honest, in that Elijah pours water where a cheat would hide fire; typical, in that the prophet trusts God to supply the flame no man can kindle. The command to soak the offering, repeated twice more in v.34, presses the precaution to its limit.
34:
The drenching is not enough for once: And he said, Do it the second time. And they did it the second time. And he said, Do it the third time. And they did it the third time. The witnesses, with only a line from John Gill — that they "poured four barrels of water more upon the wood" each time — read the threefold command as the deliberate multiplying of a precaution already sufficient. What one drenching had made unlikely, three make impossible.

The point of the verse lies in its very repetition, and the witnesses draw it from the surrounding argument. Adam Clarke's reasoning on the water governs here too: since a single soaking "must have extinguished most effectually" any fire concealed beneath, the second and third only pile proof on proof, until "this very precaution has for ever put this miracle beyond the reach of suspicion." Twelve barrels in all, poured over sacrifice and wood and running down to fill the trench, leave no crevice dry and no fraud conceivable. The prophet who gave Baal's priests the whole day now spends his own care not on hastening the answer but on removing, three times over, every possible objection to it.

The threefold drenching also throws into relief the calm of the man who orders it. Where the priests of Baal had leaped and screamed and gashed themselves through the long hours, Elijah stands unhurried, commanding water where they had begged for fire, so composed that he can afford to make the miracle harder before he makes it happen. There is, in the measured do it the second time... do it the third time, the confidence of one who knows the outcome and therefore courts the strictest scrutiny. The number three, some of the older expositors observed, lends the act a certain solemnity, as of a thing done fully and finally; but the witnesses press chiefly the plain apologetic force. By the time the water runs round the altar and brims the trench in v.35, the ground itself has become a standing witness that whatever fire may fall will fall from heaven alone, kindled by no hand and fed by no hidden flame, so that the answer, when it comes, can be laid only to the God of Israel.
35:
The precaution reaches its visible climax: And the water ran round about the altar; and he filled the trench also with water. The witnesses, working from John Gill's brief note that "there being such a large effusion of it," the water flooded down and stood in the channel, read the drenched altar as the finished proof that the coming fire can owe nothing to human contrivance. The trench dug in v.32 now does its appointed work, catching and holding the flood so that it cannot drain away but remains, a moat of water about a soaking sacrifice.

The wonder of the scene, in the eyes of the witnesses, is heightened by the drought that frames it. This is water poured out with abandon in a land where, after three rainless years, water was the most precious thing on earth; twelve barrels of it are spent not to quench thirst but to make a miracle unanswerable. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the supply was near to hand — Charles Ellicott, examining the locality, points to "a perennial spring in the neighbourhood of the traditional scene of the sacrifice, which is never known to fail in the severest drought," and, with Josephus, judges that the water "was drawn" thence rather than carried up from the distant sea. The very availability of that unfailing spring, in a country otherwise parched, becomes part of the providence of the day.

The verse, slight as it is, is the last stroke of preparation before prayer, and the witnesses let it stand as such. Everything a sceptic might allege has been forestalled: the altar is of the Lord's own ancient stones, the sacrifice is honestly dressed, no fire has been laid beneath, and now the whole is drowned in water that stands visibly in the trench for every eye to see. The prophet has done all that man can do to make the answer impossible by natural means; what remains can be done by God alone. In the flooded altar and the brimming trench the witnesses see faith's careful stagecraft brought to its finish, so that when Elijah lifts his voice at the evening hour in v.36, the eyes of all Israel are fixed on a sacrifice that only fire from heaven could ever consume.
36:
At the appointed hour Elijah prays: at the time of the offering of the evening sacrifice, Elijah the prophet came near, and said, Lord God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and that I have done all these things at thy word. The witnesses hear in the invocation both covenant and self-effacement. Adam Clarke notes why the patriarchs are named: Elijah "thus addressed the Supreme Being, that they might know, when the answer was given, that it was the same God whom the patriarchs and their fathers worshipped, and thus have their hearts turned back again to the true religion of their ancestors."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on the names of God. The prayer unites Jehovah, "describing God as He is in Himself — the One eternal self-existent Being," with the covenant title that binds him to "Abraham, and Isaac, and Israel." Ellicott marks that Elijah's whole aim is self-erasure: "He desires to efface himself. The God of Israel is to show Himself as the true worker, not only in the outer sphere by miracle, but in the inner sphere by that conversion of the hearts of the people, which to the prophet's eye is already effected." Like his New Testament antitype, "Elijah is but a voice calling on men to prepare the way of the Lord."

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the prayer's timing and its pleas. Though far from the temple, Elijah "chose the appointed time of the offering of the evening sacrifice, thereby to testify his communion with the altar at Jerusalem"; and though he expected an answer by fire, "he came near to the altar with boldness, and feared not that fire." Henry finds two pleas woven through the petition.
