First, the glory of God — "that it may be known that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and do all that I have done as thy agent, at thy word" (compare Moses' appeal, Numbers 16:28-29); Elijah "sought not his own glory but in subserviency to God's, and for his own necessary vindication." Second, "the edification of the people," that their hearts might be turned back. The witnesses do not divide; together they read the prayer as the exact opposite of Baal's clamour — short, calm, and composed, grounded in the covenant and bent wholly on God's honour and the people's return, which the doubled cry of v.37 presses home.
37:
The prayer rises to its urgent close: Hear me, O Lord, hear me, that this people may know that thou art the Lord God, and that thou hast turned their heart back again. The witnesses read the doubled cry as importunity and the closing clause as the prophet's deepest aim. John Gill marks the repetition: it is made "to express his importunity, and the vehement earnest desire of his soul to be heard in such a case, which so much concerned the glory of God." The twice-spoken hear me is not vain repetition but the pressure of a heart that stakes everything on the answer.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the petition in the frame of what the fire will prove: through the coming miracle "Jehovah not only accredited Elijah as His servant and prophet, but proved Himself to be the living God" over against the dumb idol whose day had ended in silence. The prayer asks for a public vindication — that this people may know — so that the ordeal shall settle, before all Israel, the one question the whole chapter has pressed: who is God.

The final clause carries the tenderest theology of the verse, and the witnesses linger on it. Elijah prays that the people may know "that thou hast turned their heart back again" — the conversion of Israel is asked of God as his own work, not credited to the miracle or the prophet. Matthew Henry had already found here the second great plea of the prayer, "the edification of the people," that they might "experience thy grace, turning their heart, by this miracle as a means, back again to thee, in order to thy return in a way of mercy to them." The fire is sought not for spectacle but for repentance; the outward wonder is to serve an inward turning. So the prophet who has laboured to make the miracle unanswerable now asks that it may not stop at conviction but reach the heart. The witnesses do not divide; together they hear in the doubled hear me the intensity of intercession, and in thou hast turned their heart back the confession that even the return of a wandering people is the gift of the God who answers, a gift the descending fire of v.38 is about to seal.
38:
The answer comes at once: Then the fire of the Lord fell, and consumed the burnt sacrifice, and the wood, and the stones, and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench. The witnesses insist first on what this fire was not. Keil and Delitzsch are emphatic: "the fire proceeding from Jehovah was not a natural flash of lightning, which could not produce any such effect, but miraculous fire falling from heaven," as at the tabernacle (Leviticus 9:24; 1 Chronicles 21:26; 2 Chronicles 7:1). Its supernatural origin was proved in that it "not only consumed the sacrifice, but also burned up the stones of the altar and the earth, and licked up the water in the trench."

The wesleyan Adam Clarke traces the burning step by step, as a proof engineered against every doubt. The fire "did not burst out from the altar," which might have left room for suspicion of concealed flame; it fell from above. Then he numbers the stages: the pieces of the sacrifice consumed first, then the wood — to show the flesh was not burned by the wood — then the twelve stones, then the very dust of the altar, and last "the water that was in the trench," entirely evaporated. Above all he marks the wonder against nature: "The action of this fire was in every case downward, contrary to the nature of all earthly and material fire." He sets beside it, as a pagan echo, Pindar's tale of the Rhodians whose fireless sacrifice was crowned by Jove with a shower of gold — "little more than a poetic account of the above transactions."

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the total consumption for its message. The fire "not only consumed the sacrifice and the wood, in token of God's acceptance, but licked up all the water in the trench, exhaling that, and drawing it up as a vapour, in order to the intended rain, which was to be the fruit of this sacrifice and prayer" (compare Psalms 135:7). And the burning of the stones and dust, he suggests, showed "it was no ordinary fire," and perhaps intimated that this altar, having served its turn, was never to be used again. The witnesses do not divide; together they let the downward, all-devouring flame stand as the plain and public verdict of heaven — the God who answers by fire has answered, and the drenched, dismembered sacrifice on Carmel is proof enough to move a whole nation to its face, as v.39 records.
39:
The fire settles the question, and the people fall: And when all the people saw it, they fell on their faces: and they said, The Lord, he is the God; the Lord, he is the God. The witnesses read the cry as the verdict of a jury that needs no deliberation. John Gill describes the prostration — "in reverence of God, astonished at the miracle wrought, ashamed of themselves and their sins, particularly their idolatry." Adam Clarke presses the exact confession, urging that the covenant name be heard in it: "we should translate the words thus, Jehovah, He is the God! Jehovah, He is the God! Baal is not the God; Jehovah alone is the God of Israel."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the scene beside its Mosaic pattern: it is "exactly as in Leviticus 9:24, at the inauguration of the sacrifices of the new Tabernacle by the fire from heaven, with the characteristic addition of the cry, Jehovah; He, and He only, is God." The falling of the fire and the falling of the people answer each other across the centuries; what began the tabernacle worship now recalls an apostate nation to it.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the confession for its worth and its limits. Here the people, "as the jury, gave in their verdict upon the trial, and they are all agreed"; the case was so plain "that they need not go from the bar to consider of their verdict." Yet Henry is careful: from the Lord, he is the God one would expect them to conclude, "If he be the God, he shall be our God, and we will serve him only"; but "some, we hope, had their hearts thus turned, though the generality of them were convinced only, not converted" — yielding to the truth that Jehovah is God, without consenting to the covenant that he should be theirs. And he lifts the moment toward a greater blessedness: "Blessed are those that have not seen what they saw, and yet have believed, and been wrought upon by it more than those that saw it" (compare John 20:29). The witnesses do not divide; together they hail the great confession of Carmel as a real and unanimous verdict for the Lord, while marking honestly that a cry wrung out by fire is not yet the converted heart Elijah had prayed for — and the reckoning with Baal's priests in v.40 follows immediately on the people's shout.
40:
The verdict is followed by execution: Take the prophets of Baal; let not one of them escape. And they took them: and Elijah brought them down to the brook Kishon, and slew them there. Here the witnesses openly wrestle with the ethics of the deed. Keil and Delitzsch justify it by the law of the theocracy: Elijah acted "not so much from revenge as to carry out the fundamental law of the Old Testament kingdom of God, which prohibited idolatry on pain of death, and commanded that false prophets should be destroyed" (Deuteronomy 13:6-18; Deuteronomy 17:2-7). Adam Clarke agrees that "their lives were forfeited to that law which had ordered every idolater to be slain," and notes that Ahab, "who was present, consented to this act."

The great division is over how a Christian should regard the slaughter. Charles Ellicott presses the discomfort with unusual candour. The "ruthless slaughter" belongs "alike to the fierce righteousness of the character of Elijah, and to the spirit of the old Law" — a law "adapted, as in the terrible example of the slaughter of the Canaanites, to the hardness of men's hearts." That law "did not recognise the difference between moral and political offences punishable by human law, and the religious sin or apostasy which we have been taught to leave to the judgment of God alone." Hence, Ellicott warns, "the frequent quotation of such examples by Christians — of which the request to call down fire is the first (Luke 9:54) — is a spiritual anachronism." Yet he grants the mitigating fact that those slain "were no doubt implicated in the persecution headed by Jezebel," so that Elijah "felt himself the avenger of the slaughtered prophets, as well as the instrument of the judgment of God."

The puritan Matthew Henry rests on the plain warrant of the law, "acting still by an extraordinary commission, which is not to be drawn into a precedent," and marks a providence in the escape of the four hundred prophets of the groves, who, though summoned in v.19, stayed at Jezebel's table: their reprieve only reserved them to become "the instruments of Ahab's destruction" at Ramoth-gilead (1 Kings 22:6). The witnesses thus divide not over the fact but over its bearing: whether the deed is a righteous execution under a divinely given law (Keil, Clarke, Henry) or a fierce act belonging to an age of hard hearts, not to be imitated under the gospel (Ellicott). All agree it flowed from the same zeal that had just called down fire, and that with idolatry's priests slain the way lay open for the mercy of rain in v.41.
41:
With Baal's priests slain, Elijah turns to the king: Get thee up, eat and drink; for there is a sound of abundance of rain. The witnesses read the word as the confidence of faith and, in one reading, as a touch of scorn. Keil and Delitzsch picture the scene plainly: Ahab, who "had eaten nothing from morning till evening in his eagerness to see the result," is bidden come up from the brook Kishon to Carmel and refresh himself, "for he (Elijah) could already hear the noise of a fall of rain" — though, the Hebrew being verbless, it is simply the sound, the noise of coming rain. Once the cause of the curse was removed by the destruction of the idolatrous priests, "the curse itself could also be removed," yet not without the prophet's word.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears an edge in the command: "There seems a touch of scorn in these words." Ahab had "descended to the place of slaughter, looking on silent — if not unmoved — while the priests whose worship he had sanctioned were slain by hundreds"; now Elijah bids him "get up to his palace, taking it for granted that, fresh from that horrible sight, he is yet ready to feast." Ellicott's summary is unsparing: "The king goes to revel, the prophet to pray." Adam Clarke, more gently, takes the invitation as evidence "that Ahab and the prophet were now on good terms," a further proof that the slaying of the priests had the king's consent.

The puritan Matthew Henry dwells on the ear of faith. Elijah bids the king "eat and drink, for joy that God had now accepted his works, and that rain was coming"; and though others perceived no sign of it, "Elijah, by faith, hears the sound of abundance of rain." Henry draws the doctrine: "God reveals his secrets to his servants the prophets; and yet, without a revelation, we may foresee that when man's judgments run down like a river, God's mercy will" — for "rain is the river of God" (Psalms 65:9). And though rain was promised, still it must be asked (Zechariah 10:1), which sends the prophet to his knees while the king goes to his table (Ecclesiastes 9:7). The witnesses do not finally divide; together they set the feasting king and the praying prophet side by side, and let the contrast prepare the wrestling intercession of v.42.
42:
The two men part to their opposite errands: So Ahab went up to eat and to drink. And Elijah went up to the top of Carmel; and he cast himself down upon the earth, and put his face between his knees. The witnesses read the prophet's posture as the very picture of importunate prayer. Keil and Delitzsch explain the going up: while the king refreshed himself, "Elijah went up to the top of Carmel to pray that the Lord would complete His work by fulfilling His promise in sending rain," and "continued in prayer" until his servant brought word of the little cloud. The bowed attitude, they note, is the one the apostle recalls when he holds up Elijah as the pattern of effectual prayer (James 5:18).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Adam Clarke describe the strange bodily form of it. Clarke pictures him kneeling and then bowing "so that, while his face was between his knees, his forehead touched the ground." Ellicott calls it "a peculiar attitude — distinct from the ordinary postures of standing and kneeling — which has been noted as existing still among the modern dervishes," and reads in it "the vehement excitement of the moment, and the impulsive nature of Elijah." The man who had stood erect and commanding before the fire now folds himself to the earth to plead for the rain.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the lesson of the lonely summit. Elijah "withdrew to a strange place, to the top of Carmel, which was very high and very private" — the same peak whose recesses the prophet Amos names as a hiding-place (Amos 9:3). There "he would be alone," and Henry draws the rule: "Those who are called to appear and act in public for God must yet find time to be private with him, and keep up their converse with him in solitude." Elijah "set himself, as it were, upon his watch-tower," like the prophet who resolved to watch and see what God would say (Habakkuk 2:1); and he abased himself in the very posture of humility and importunity now that God had so honoured him. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the feasting king below and the prostrate prophet above, and find in the face bowed between the knees the earnestness that, refusing to rise until it prevails, sends the servant to the sea in v.43.
43:
The prayer becomes a vigil: Go up now, look toward the sea. And he went up, and looked, and said, There is nothing. And he said, Go again seven times. The witnesses read the sevenfold sending as the great lesson of persevering prayer. Adam Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch fix the vantage — "from the top of Mount Carmel the Mediterranean Sea was full in view" — so that the servant had only to climb the last height to scan the horizon for the first sign of change. Six times he brings back the flat report, there is nothing; still the prophet will not stir from his knees.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes this the classic text on delayed answer. "From this delay of the answer to prayer Elijah's example became proverbial for intensity and perseverance in supplication" (James 5:17). And he marks a striking contrast within the same chapter: "the immediate answer to his earlier prayer" for fire, granted at once "for the sake of the people," set against "the long delay here," which he judges was "for some lesson — perhaps of humility and patience — to Elijah himself." When at last the answer comes, "it fulfils itself speedily."

The puritan Matthew Henry expounds the discipline of the waiting. Six times the servant goes and "sees nothing, brings no good news to his master; yet Elijah continues praying," refusing even to go and look with his own eyes, but keeping "his mind close and intent in prayer," like one who has taken up "his father Jacob's resolution, I will not let thee go except thou bless me" (Genesis 32:26). Henry draws the application for every believer: "Though the answer of our fervent and believing supplications may not come quickly, yet we must continue instant in prayer, and not faint nor desist; for at the end it shall speak, and not lie." The witnesses do not divide; together they read the seven trips to the summit as a parable of importunity — the promise sure, the fulfilment sought, and the prophet holding on through six empty reports until, at the seventh, the little cloud of v.44 at last appears above the sea.
44:
At the seventh look the sign appears: Behold, there ariseth a little cloud out of the sea, like a man's hand. And he said, Go up, say unto Ahab, Prepare thy chariot, and get thee down, that the rain stop thee not. The witnesses read the little cloud as the great truth of small beginnings. Adam Clarke attends to the image, rendering the Hebrew "like the hollow of a man's hand — in the form of the hand bent, the concave side downmost." He adds his own observation of such a cloud at sea "previously to a violent storm," and Bruce's account from Abyssinia of a small whirling cloud that swiftly "extends itself greatly" and gathers the vapours from every quarter — a sight that "put me always in mind of Elijah foretelling rain on Mount Carmel."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the swiftness and the practical urgency of the message: the prophet at once bids Ahab harness and descend from Carmel to his chariot at the mountain's foot, that the coming downpour "stop thee not" — for the dusty, sun-baked plain would soon turn to mud beneath the wheels. The word of command is as prompt as the cloud is small.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the doctrine the verse was made for. A cloud "no bigger than a man's hand" is enough, for "great blessings often arise from small beginnings, and showers of plenty from a cloud of a span long." He turns it into counsel: "Let us therefore never despise the day of small things, but hope and wait for great things from it" (compare Zechariah 4:10). And he presses the likeness into the whole work of grace: "Scarcely to be perceived are the first workings of his Spirit in the heart, which grow up at last to the wonder of men, and applause of angels." What the eye can barely catch on the horizon is the beginning of the flood that will break the three years' drought. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in the hand-sized cloud the token of a great mercy scarcely begun, and let the prophet's urgent charge to the king carry the scene forward to the bursting storm of v.45.
45:
The little cloud keeps its promise with speed: in the mean while, the heaven was black with clouds and wind, and there was a great rain. And Ahab rode, and went to Jezreel. The witnesses read the swift storm as the abundant mercy that answered so small a sign. Keil and Delitzsch catch the suddenness in the Hebrew idiom — hither and thither, that is, "while the hand is being moved to and fro, very speedily" — so that "before any provision had been made for it, the heaven turned black with storm-clouds, and there came a great fall of rain," while Ahab drove the road to Jezreel. They add the topography: from the foot of Carmel the king could just reach Jezreel, "the present Zerin," before the downpour overtook him.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the first appearance of the city as a royal seat. Jezreel, "a city of Issachar" (Joshua 19:18), bore a name signifying "Jehovah hath sown," applied to "the whole of the rich plain, the garden and battlefield of northern Palestine." Made a royal residence by Ahab as Samaria was by Omri, it stood "in a position of some strength and great beauty, supplied by unfailing springs, visible from Carmel." To that fair, well-watered plain, so long scorched, the king now drives through the first rain in three years and a half.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the quality of the rain against the false starts of a hollow religion. This was "not as a morning cloud, which passes away" — though, he adds sadly, "Israel's goodness was so" — "but one that produced a plentiful rain, and an earnest of more" (compare Psalms 68:9). The drought that judged the nation's idolatry is broken in a single evening; the God who answered by fire now answers by water, and the blessing withheld so long is poured out without stint. The witnesses do not divide; together they read the blackening sky and the great rain as the visible token of a favour restored, mercy following hard upon reformation, and they let the king's ride to Jezreel through the storm set the stage for the last and strangest scene of the chapter, the prophet running before the chariot in v.46.
46:
The chapter closes on a marvel of strength and humility: And the hand of the Lord was on Elijah; and he girded up his loins, and ran before Ahab to the entrance of Jezreel. The witnesses read the running for its divine power and its lowly grace, and divide gently over what it chiefly means. John Gill, with the Targum, takes "the hand of the Lord" as a special endowment of body and spirit — "the spirit of strength from the Lord was with him" — enabling the prophet to outstrip the king's horses.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke dismisses the notion that Elijah ran as a mere herald chanting the king's praises: "I believe all these entirely mistake the writer's meaning." Rather, "the hand of the Lord, or, as the Targum says, the spirit of strength, came upon Elijah, and he girded up his loins" — tucked his long garments into his girdle — "and ran; and notwithstanding the advantage the king had by means of his chariot, the prophet reached Jezreel before him." All this, Clarke holds, "was intended to show that he was under the peculiar influence and inspiration of the Almighty, that the king might respect and fear him."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott read the deed as humility more than power. Keil finds Elijah, "whom Ahab had hitherto only known as a stern and powerful prophet," now showing "himself to be his faithful subject and servant," running before the chariot "to give him a proof of his humility, and thus deepen the impression made upon his heart, and fortify him against the temptations of Jezebel." Ellicott sees "a striking reaction of enthusiastic thankfulness after the stern calmness of his whole attitude"; the "frenzied excitement seems strange" for a prophet, and "could not have belonged to a Samuel, an Elisha, or an Isaiah," but "in the simple and enthusiastic warrior of God it is natural enough." The puritan Matthew Henry notes that Ahab, unlike the eunuch with Philip, never invited the prophet into his chariot; so Elijah "ran before him as one of his footmen," that "God's ministers should make it appear that, how great soever they look when they deliver God's message, yet they are far from affecting worldly grandeur." The witnesses do not finally divide; together they end the chapter with the same man who called down fire now girding his robe and running in the rain before a king — power and lowliness in one figure, the servant of the Lord of hosts.
